title: Bot Fix: Infinite Loop / Oscillation and Starvation Timer
content:
## Problem

In Iron Snake mode (and occasionally classic mode), AI snakes get permanently stuck in approach-and-retreat cycles: a bot paths toward food, correctly refuses to enter a tight pocket that would trap it, retreats, then deterministically re-selects the exact same losing approach and repeats forever. The game never ends and the bot never makes progress.

**Root cause:** Every bot (`src/bots/*.ts`) is a stateless, per-tick, greedy direction-scorer with no memory, no randomness, and no tie-breaking. Given an identical board configuration, scoring produces an identical choice, so any positional cycle is stable and never breaks. Iron Snake's irregular board masks create far more tight pockets, making the problem especially visible there. This is inherent to the architecture, not a bug in any individual bot.

A secondary problem: even after the loop guard was added, a bot that wanders productively but never reaches food could keep the game running indefinitely, requiring manual intervention.

---

## Solution 1 — Loop Guard (`src/bots/loop-guard.ts`)

### Design decision: harness-level guard, not per-bot changes

All 11 bots are stateless pure functions returning only a `Direction`. Adding memory to each would require changing the shared bot interface and rewriting every bot. Instead, a centralized guard at the harness level intercepts the chosen direction only when a loop is actually detected, leaving all bot personalities completely intact during normal play.

### Detection: two signals

A revisit counter alone proved insufficient. A pure revisit counter (3 hits in a window) cannot catch a ~20-cell "approach, give up, wander back" circuit — on a period-20 loop, no cell recurs 3 times until 60 moves have passed. Two signals are used together:

1. **Tight oscillation:** any cell revisited 3+ times in a 40-move rolling window of recent head positions.
2. **Confinement ratio:** too few distinct cells relative to total moves over a 24-move sample — catches larger circuits that the revisit counter alone misses. Confirmed by simulation: the guard broke a bot out of a 20-cell ring to explore 125 cells.

### Escape behavior

When a loop is detected, `chooseEscapeDirection` picks the legal, non-trapping move whose destination cell has been visited least recently, with random tie-breaking. The escape direction is committed for **8 ticks** (`escapeTicks`) so the snake does not snap back immediately. Falls back to max `reachableArea` if no tail-safe move exists; returns `null` if no safe move is available (harness keeps the bot's original pick).

All logic uses the existing `BotHelpers` (`simulateMove`, `analyzePosition`, `getCandidateDirections`), so it automatically respects the Iron Snake `boardMask` and two-player opponent-blocking helpers.

### Exported API

```ts
type LoopMemory = { recentHeads: string[]; escapeTicks: number }
createLoopMemory(): LoopMemory
resetLoopMemory(mem: LoopMemory): void
recordHead(mem: LoopMemory, head: Position): void
isLooping(mem: LoopMemory): boolean
chooseEscapeDirection(state, helpers, mem): Direction | null
```

### Harness wiring (`src/main.ts`)

- Per-snake `LoopMemory` fields: `botLoopMemory` (P1/single-player) and `botLoopMemory2` (P2), initialized with `createLoopMemory()`.
- In `chooseBotDirection` and `chooseBotDirectionForPlayer`: after the bot picks its direction, `recordHead` is called; if `escapeTicks > 0` or `isLooping()` is true, `chooseEscapeDirection` is called and its result replaces the bot's pick. `escapeTicks` is set to 8 on escape entry and decremented each tick.
- `resetLoopMemory` is called at every progress point: food eaten (single- and two-player), `advanceLevel`, `repositionSnakes`, and all game start/restart paths.

### Menu demo (`src/menu-demo.ts`)

The title-screen background animation has its own bot harness. The same `loop-guard` module is wired there (one `LoopMemory` per demo snake), eliminating oscillation from the menu visuals without duplicating logic.

---

## Solution 2 — Starvation Timer (`src/main.ts`)

### Design decision: area-based budget, not length-based

The move budget scales with the playable board area rather than snake length. A bigger board means food can legitimately be farther away, so the limit grows with the board. A competent snake never approaches the limit; it fires only as a backstop when food is genuinely unreachable.

**Formula:** `starvationLimit() = max(100, 2 × boardArea)`

A latent bug was also fixed: `expandGrid()` doubled `gridSize` without refreshing `boardArea`, which would have made the starvation limit stale after a classic-mode grid expansion.

### Mechanics

- Per-snake move counters `movesSinceFood1` and `movesSinceFood2` increment each tick and reset to zero when that snake eats.
- When a counter reaches `starvationLimit()`, the snake is declared dead via the existing game-over paths — single-player calls `endGame()`; two-player feeds the starved snake as "dead" into `endGameTwoPlayer()`. Two-player starvation draws (both snakes starving on the same tick) are handled for free by the existing symmetry in `endGameTwoPlayer`.

### Design decision: reuse existing loss paths

Starvation is routed through the same `endGame` / `endGameTwoPlayer` calls already used for collision deaths, with a `deathCause` flag to adjust displayed text. Win/loss/draw resolution logic is untouched.

**Game-over text variants:**
- Single-player: `"Starved!"`
- Two-player: `"Player 2 Wins! (P1 starved)"`, `"Draw — both starved!"`, etc.

### Visual hunger warning

- Once a snake passes **60% of its move budget** without eating, its colours blend toward an amber/red warning palette via a `lerpHex()` colour interpolator in `draw()`, ramping to full warning colour at the limit.
- A pulsing overlay banner (`#starvation-warning` in `index.html`, styled in `src/style.css`) appears the instant the hunger tint begins, naming which snake is at risk in two-player modes. It is positioned over the canvas via a `.canvas-wrap` wrapper so it does not shift the game layout.
- A plain urgent message was chosen over a live countdown to avoid per-tick number flicker.

---

## Files Changed

| File | Role |
|---|---|
| `src/bots/loop-guard.ts` | New module: loop detection + escape logic |
| `src/main.ts` | Loop guard wiring, starvation counters, `starvationLimit()`, `boardArea` fix |
| `src/menu-demo.ts` | Loop guard wiring for title-screen demo snakes |
| `index.html` | `#starvation-warning` banner element, `.canvas-wrap` wrapper |
| `src/style.css` | Pulsing pill styles for starvation warning overlay |
